SMEs & Circular Service Providers (SCSP) Recycling SMEs; logistics operators; digital service providers; waste processors Integration into ≥3 circular value chains; new business models for collection, traceability and processing; access to DST tools; local green job creation. Municipalities & Waste Management Authorities (MWA) City councils; municipal waste operators; environmental agencies Establishment of pilot collection scheme; ≥10% increase in paint return rates; reduced illegal dumping; data-driven planning through DST. Citizens & Society (CSS) Households; professionals; vulnerable groups; youth; women Safer disposal pathways addressing stockpiled paints; access to digital platform and incentives; increased participation in circular practices; reduced exposure to improper storage and disposal risks. Expected Outcomes Outcome 1: Prove the technical, economic, and environmental feasibility … net zero technologies and components We will demonstrate four pilot lines (MW /US, PEF, MNPs, SAMS) at TRL 7, showing how spoiled paints and sludges can be reprocessed into valuable secondary raw materials. These pilots are embedded in real industrial environments: PPG will host the best performing pilot processing line based on performance KPIs (microbial reduction, recovery yield, energy efficiency), while CRF and Farplas will provide real industrial paint sludges (overspray) for SAMS treatment. Recovered pigments, fillers, and binders are either looped back into paints or channelled into energy-intensive industries such as cement, mortars, ceramics and polymer-based construction products, directly supplying value chains relevant to net-zero technologies (e.g. low-carbon cement, energy-efficient building materials, advanced coatings). Technical feasibility is demonstrated through pilot-scale validation (TRL 7) under industrial conditions, including continuous operation, process stability and product quality compliance. Economic feasibility is ensured through reduced disposal costs, recovery of high-value materials (e.g. TiO₂, mineral fillers), and integration into existing industrial value chains. Environmental feasibility is validated through lifecycle-based performance, including CO₂ reduction via material substitution and avoidance of incineration. This validates cross-sectoral circular value chains linking coating sector wastes with construction and ceramics, reducing dependence on virgin raw materials and supporting net-zero industrial systems. Outcome 2: Enable doubling the ratio of secondary raw materials … compared to present levels; Today, residue rates in industrial painting can reach 40 –50% of raw materials use, with most treated as waste. We target ≥70% recovery of pigments and fillers and ≥50% from what is now spoiled paint or sludge, turning a liability into feedstock. This is enabled through the graded routing approach (A –C), allowing reuse, reformulation and cross-sector valorisation of recovered materials. In real terms, a single automotive facility that currently generates ~240 t paint residues annually could recover ~ 170 t of valuable secondary raw materials, significantly increasing resource efficiency compared to current practices dominated by incineration or downcycling. Downstream, even modest substitution rates have major impacts: a 5% cement clinker replacement with Ca/Si-rich paint residues translates into ~40 kg CO₂ saved per tonne of cement (average clinker footprint ~800 kg CO₂/t). Additional gains in energy efficiency are achieved by avoiding primary raw material production and thermal disposal routes. By validating ≥30% substitution in new paints, ≥10% in cement and ceramics and ≥5% in polymer tiles, we demonstrate how material loops can increase secondary raw material use while improving resource efficiency, energy efficiency and carbon lifecycle performance across energy-intensive sectors. At EU scale, this approach can convert large volumes of paint residues into secondary raw materials, significantly contributing to resource efficiency and emissions reduction across energy-intensive industries. Outcome 3: Increase the competitiveness of the European … net zero technologies and components We create new circular business models linking paints, construction, ceramics, and recycling industries, strengthening the competitiveness of the European process industry. The digital marketplace goes beyond industrial actors, engaging Industry, Retailers, and Citizens in collection, tracking, and valorisation of paint residues. This enables the conversion of waste streams into certified secondary raw materials. This generates new revenue streams through material substitution and reduced disposal costs, particularly benefiting SMEs in logistics, recycling, digital services and reformulation, which are embedded as key actors in the value chain. By validating recovered pigments and fillers in paints, cement, ceramics and polymer-based products, we supply secondary raw materials to net-zero value chains, including low-carbon construction materials and advanced coatings. At scale, this approach can unlock €100M+ valorisation potential (as analyzed in paragraph 2.2.B.5) while creating new business opportunities and service-based models for SMEs across circular industrial ecosystems. Outcome 4: Foster the use of digital tools … digital assets principles. Our project’s digital platform and Decision Support Tool (DST) ensure end-to-end traceability, linking waste generators (households, workshops, manufacturers) to reprocessing lines and downstream industries. By integrating DPP standards and aligning with ECHA databases, the system guarantees compliance, transparency, and secure data sharing across the value chain. All datasets —batch composition, microbial stability, recovery efficiency —will be managed under FAIR principles, creating open, trusted digital assets. This establishes a scalable digital backbone for circular industrial ecosystems, applicable beyond paints to other resource-intensive value chains. Also, we will foster DPP adoption for markets like ceramics. Outcome 5: As appropriate in one or more projects, … and upgrading of industries of Ukraine. We demonstrate replication across European and associated markets, with a specific focus on Ukraine’s industrial recovery. Our Ukrainian partner SBT operating in the construction sector, and more specifically in the production of polymeric tiles and components, will test recovered fractions (i.e. oxides and resins) for fillers in its local production lines. This sector is of particular importance for Ukraine’s reconstruction. Thus, we validate the technology under different market conditions and create a template for circular reconstruction pathways in Ukraine. By reducing reliance on imported raw materials and introducing EU-aligned digital traceability, we strengthen industrial resilience and accelerate integration of Ukrainian industries into climate-neutral European value chains. It then identifies the target groups that will benefit, and the structural barriers that we must address to convert demonstrated TRL 6 performance into market uptake. 

2.1.1 Contribution to topic outcomes and wider destination impacts The market context in which our INFUSE results will land is rapidly expanding. The global HEA market was valued at approximately USD 1.2 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow at a compound annual rate of around 7–8 %, reaching USD 2.4 billion by 2034 (with some analyst estimates extending to USD 4.6 billion by 2032 depending on scope 2). Aerospace and defence consistently represent the largest end-use segment, driven by demand for materials that combine lightweight characteristics with high mechanical strength and oxidation resistance at elevated temperatures. The broader metal additive manufacturing market stood at approximately USD 4.6 billion in 2024, with the aerospace metal AM sub-segment alone valued at approximately USD 1.2 billion and projected to reach USD 3.5 billion by 2033 at a CAGR of 14.5 %. In Europe, LPBF commands approximately 50.7 % of the metal AM market, confirming LPBF as the industrially dominant route in which our powder circularity and process-window knowledge will have the highest leverage. The growing performance monitoring segment for powertrains, served by Demonstrator DEM3 (HEA thin-film sensors on flexible substrates for turbine and automotive SHM), is projected to reach USD 4.2 billion globally by 2030, with aerospace and automotive SHM accounting for over 40% of demand.

2.1 Project’s pathways towards impact (a) Contribution to (1) the outcomes specified in the call topic and (2) the wider impacts specified in the destination of the work programme H2SAVE is geared towards a clear and credible pathway towards impact by translating advances in reversible protonic ceramic electrochemical cell (PCEC) technology into a validated, system-level energy storage solution addressing the resilience, decarbonisation and security needs of Europe’s critical infrastructures. Beyond the project duration, we expect our results to support the progressive replacement of fossil-based backup systems, enable long-duration off-grid energy storage, and strengthen European technological leadership in clean energy systems. We will contribute directly to the objectives of European policies supporting energy system resilience, decarbonisation, and sector coupling. By integrating hydrogen technologies with advanced energy management systems and digital modelling capabilities, our proposed solution supports the development of flexible, low-carbon energy infrastructures capable of operating under both normal and emergency conditions. Our system contributes to the call scope by enabling: ▪ increased resilience of energy systems against large-scale grid disruptions ▪ integration of renewable energy sources through hydrogen-based energy storage ▪ flexible interaction with electricity grids through ancillary service provision ▪ improved energy autonomy for critical infrastructures and distributed energy systems. Our hybrid architecture allows the system to operate efficiently in grid-connected mode, where surplus capacity can provide flexibility services, and in islanded mode, where we ensure energy supply during outages. In addition, we contribute to the deployment of hydrogen-based distributed energy systems, supporting the broader European Hydrogen Strategy and enabling scalable applications across buildings, industrial facilities, and community energy systems. Contribution of our project results to the call topic outcomes: Our project results will contribute to all the expected outcomes listed in the HORIZON-CL5-2026-03-D3-21 call topic by advancing a reversible hydrogen-based energy storage system tailored to critical infrastructures, with validation at TRL5 under representative operating conditions. Enhanced European knowledge and leadership in off-grid clean energy storage technologies. H2SAVE advances reversible PCEC-based hydrogen storage from lab-scale to TRL5 system validation, reinforcing European leadership in long-duration, off-grid clean energy storage solutions. We will deliver validated designs, models, and operational data for reversible PCEC, an emerging technology that converts energy between power and hydrogen in which Europe could build a strategic positioning. H2SAVE will generate high-quality experimental datasets (>1000 hours of integrated system operation), validated Multiphysics models (±10% electrical / ±25 °C thermal accuracy), and Digital Twin frameworks enabling predictive operation. These results will strengthen Europe’s leadership in intermediate-temperature electrochemical systems (500–600 °C) and hybrid hydrogen–battery architectures, providing a technological basis for future TRL6–7 scale-up and industrial deployment. The rapid growth of the off-grid energy storage market, currently estimated at ~USD 20 billion in 2025 and projected to exceed USD 80 billion by 2034 (CAGR ~16–17%), reflects increasing demand for resilient and decentralised energy solutions. However, this market is currently dominated by battery-based systems, which are constrained in duration, scalability, and performance under long-duration outage scenarios. For example, while lithium-ion batteries are excellent for short-term balancing, typically up to four hours, they cannot cover multi-day weather events or seasonal shifts. H2SAVE has the potential to fill this gap by introducing hydrogen-based storage capable of delivering multi-day autonomy and enhanced resilience, which could position Europe to take the lead in the next generation of off-grid energy storage solutions. Primary target groups who will benefit: European research organisations, electrochemical system manufacturers, hydrogen technology developers, and system integrators. Improved energy security and cost-effectiveness of Europe's energy systems, particularly for critical infrastructure. Despite the high reliability standards of European power systems, multiple events in the past have put grids to the test. In the face of such events, up to 55% of Europe’s power system has limited options to import electricity, increasing the risk of blackouts across the continent. Spain, Ireland and Finland are particularly exposed, with very little support available from neighbours in case of grid incidents. For example, on 28th April 2025, one of the largest electricity blackouts in European history occurred, leaving around 60 million people across Spain and Portugal without power for almost 24 hours. With around 40% of the EU’s electricity grid being over 40 years old and nearing the end of its operational life, and in light of the increasing risks posed by climate change, conflicts, sabotage, and with the rapid growth of renewable energy usage (which presents a fundamental operational characteristic of variability), there is an urgent requirement to develop stabilising systems (technologies and infrastructure to maintain the balance, reliability and resilience of the electricity grid), in particular for energy storage. Indeed, experts state that the EU should create a framework to support investment in storage technologies, from lithium-ion batteries to pumped storage power plants and hydrogen storage, which will reduce the risk of further sudden power outages. The H2SAVE system will operate flexibly across grid-connected and islanded conditions, operating both as an electrolyser producing hydrogen for storage and as a fuel cell generating electricity when needed. To this end, beyond backup functionality, we expect that H2SAVE will provide services to the grid and participate in balancing markets, whereby the fuel cell would not be used only for energy supply, but also for grid support. This dual operational capability will strengthen energy security for critical infrastructures while enabling more efficient utilisation of assets. Indeed, innovations such as H2SAVE can play a vital role in contributing to energy stability for mitigating outage risks, which is vital in that energy stability is linked to the operation of a host of other critical infrastructures- hospitals, data centres, transportation; critical for civilian life, but also for military response; and energy infrastructure remains a major target, as shown by examples from Ukraine and Russia’s hybrid warfare campaign in Europe. H2SAVE provides reliable, long-duration and autonomous backup power for critical infrastructures, reducing exposure to grid failures and lowering lifecycle costs compared to diesel-based solutions. By targeting applications where diesel generators are largely the default solution, H2SAVE directly supports the progressive elimination of fossil-based backup power in sensitive environments. We will deliver validated operational concepts, safety-by-design documentation (including HAZID/HAZOP-informed guidelines), and hybridisation strategies combining hydrogen storage with batteries for fast response and long-duration resilience. These outputs will lower barriers to adoption and accelerate the transition towards zero-emission backup solutions. H2SAVE targets resilient, off-grid energy supply for critical infrastructures by combining long-duration hydrogen storage with fast-response battery systems. Our system is designed to sustain critical loads during extended outages (multi-day autonomy) and ensure controlled operation under both grid-connected and islanded conditions. Cost-effectiveness is addressed through system design and validation, supported by Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), targeting competitive cost structures relative to diesel-based backup solutions. We will quantify system performance in terms of availability (>95% operational availability during test campaigns), efficiency, and cost per kWh stored, providing evidence for economically viable deployment. Primary target groups who will benefit: Critical infrastructure operators, public authorities responsible for emergency preparedness, and European technology providers seeking alternatives to diesel-based systems. Elimination of fossil-based backup solutions for critical infrastructures, strengthening European competitiveness. It is estimated that there are around 82.6 million diesel generators in operation worldwide. The 2022 global market size for diesel generators reached USD 17.10 billion and is projected to witness a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 9.4% from 2023 to 2032. Their emissions contribute significantly to environmental pollution and pose health risks. In recent years, global efforts have focused on mitigating the negative effects of diesel engine emissions on human health and the environment. Considerable amounts of CO2 and other harmful gases such as nitrogen oxide (NOX), sulphur oxide (SOX), carbon monoxide (CO), hydrocarbons (HC) and particulate matter (PM) are emitted by diesel generators. H2SAVE represents a next-generation hydrogen energy storage (HES) system, leveraging reversible protonic ceramic electrochemical technology to overcome limitations of conventional electrolyser–fuel cell configurations. It aims to replace diesel generators with a zero-emission hydrogen-based system, improving air quality, reducing noise, and strengthening the competitiveness of European clean energy technologies. In the case of the pilot hospital, their energy demand is typically 0.8–2 MW. Considering that the H2SAVE system produces 500 kW, we thereby provide partial backup aligned with the targeted supply of critical loads (ICU, OR, emergency) in hospital environments (rather than full facility demand), ensuring realistic deployment and scalability. In addition, the H2SAVE system produces ⁓60 Nm³/h O₂, thereby producing oxygen that can be used in hospital applications, such as intensive care and surgery. The H2SAVE system can produce 500 kW of the 0.8–2 MW oxygen demand of the hospital. By targeting applications where diesel generators remain the default solution, H2SAVE directly supports the progressive elimination of fossil-based backup power in sensitive environments. We deliver validated operational concepts, safety-by-design documentation (including HAZID/HAZOP-informed guidelines), and hybridisation strategies combining hydrogen storage with batteries for fast response and long-duration resilience. These outputs lower barriers to adoption and accelerate the transition towards zero-emission backup solutions. H2SAVE provides a technically validated pathway to replace diesel generators in critical infrastructures with hydrogen-based systems. We will demonstrate diesel-free backup operation under representative conditions, including blackout scenarios and emergency response testing (≥4 resilience scenarios). By enabling reliable, safe, and controllable operation over extended periods (>500 hours of testing), we support the transition towards zero-emission backup systems, reducing CO₂ emissions and dependency on fossil fuels while strengthening European industrial competitiveness
